message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    SARS-CoV2 has evolved to enter the host via the ACE2 receptor which is part of the Kinin-kallirein pathway. This complex pathway is only poorly understood in context of immune regulation but critical to control infection. This study examines SARS-CoV2 infection and epithelial mechanisms of the kinin-kallikrein system at the kinin B
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    receptor level in SARS-CoV-2 infection that is of direct translational relevance.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    From acute SARS-CoV-2-positive patients and -negative controls, transcriptomes of nasal brushings were analyzed. Primary airway epithelial cells (NHBEs) were infected with SARS-CoV-2 and treated with the approved B
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    R antagonist icatibant. SARS-CoV-2 RNA RT-qPCR, cytotoxicity assays, plaque assays and transcriptome analyses were performed. The treatment effect was further studied in a murine airway inflammation model
                    <jats:italic>in vivo</jats:italic>
                    .
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Here, we report a broad and strong upregulation of kallikreins and the kinin B
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    receptor (B
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    R) in the nasal mucosa of acutely symptomatic SARS-CoV-2-positive patients. A B
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    R antagonist impeded SARS-CoV-2 replication and spread in NHBEs, as determined in plaque assays on Vero E6 cells. B
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    R antagonism reduced the expression of SARS-CoV-2 entry receptor ACE2
                    <jats:italic>in vitro</jats:italic>
                    and in a murine airway inflammation model
                    <jats:italic>in vivo</jats:italic>
                    . In addition, it suppressed gene expression broadly, particularly genes involved in G-protein-coupled-receptor signaling and ion transport.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    In summary, this study provides evidence that treatment with B
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    R antagonists protects airway epithelial cells from SARS-CoV-2 by inhibiting its replication and spread, through the reduction of ACE2 levels and the interference with several cellular signaling processes. Future clinical studies need to shed light on the airway protection potential of approved B
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    R antagonists, like icatibant, in the treatment of early-stage COVID-19.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title:
Early Reduction of SARS-CoV-2 Replication in Bronchial Epithelium by Kinin B
                  <sub>2</sub>
                  Receptor Antagonism
